cataloging_subject.label: English Conquest of Ireland
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is one anonymous late-medieval Hiberno-Middle English prose translation and free adaptation of Gerald of Wales's Latin Expugnatio Hibernica. EngConIre represents that work in two parallel manuscript versions printed on facing pages by Frederick J. Furnivall and serialized sequentially in the University of Michigan XML. The record does not identify the anonymous English translator with Gerald of Wales, Thomas Bray, James Yonge, any manuscript scribe, Furnivall, either physical codex, the 1896 or 1969 edition, the Michigan encoding, or the General Corpus XML artifact. Furnivall's preliminaries, his specimen from a third manuscript, and his terminal note are retained as separately labeled editorial paratext, not counted as additional medieval works.

catalog_summary.title: English Conquest of Ireland
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter
catalog_summary.date: Translation date unknown; currently attested by MS. Rawl. B. 490, dated 1400–1410, and therefore composed no later than 1410
catalog_summary.region: Late medieval Ireland and its English colonial community; exact place of composition unknown

titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloging, following the established edition and catalog title
notes: No authorial title for the lost translation has been verified; this concise discovery title derives from the editorial and catalog tradition.

titles.value: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166–1185
notes: This is the 1896 edition title and supplies the work's conventional modern title and historical range; it is not claimed as a manuscript or authorial title.

agents.name: Mrs Parker
notes: Furnivall names her only as Mrs Parker when crediting the working copy of MS. Rawl. B. 490; a fuller identity was not verified.

agents.name: Thomas Bray
notes: Recorded only because the current TCD MS 592 description titles the work 'Conquest of Ireland by Thomas Bray'; no independent evidence verified that attribution.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translator and adapter

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship treats the English text as an anonymous translation and adaptation; Gerald of Wales authored the Latin source, not this English wording.
The vernacular work abridges, rearranges, omits, and expands its Latin source rather than reproducing it word for word.

scope.description: Furnivall's 1896 two-witness parallel-text edition and its retained editorial framing
Transcription of the TCD MS 592 main version and the short TCD MS 593 comparison specimen
Working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490 used by Furnivall
Collation of the edition proofs against MS. Rawl. B. 490
Current catalog wording for TCD MS 592, retained only as attribution history

assertion.notes: The present record does not accept Thomas Bray as author. The catalog wording conflicts with the anonymous-work treatment in modern scholarship and has not been independently substantiated.

notes: James Yonge is not assigned any responsibility for this work: his Secreta Secretorum translation begins as adjacent content in MS 592 and is separately present in MS. Rawl. B. 490.

date_statements.display: Translation date unknown; composed no later than 1410 on the currently verified evidence

assertion.notes: The upper bound is inferred from the current 1400–1410 date of MS. Rawl. B. 490, not from a dated authorial exemplar. Furnivall's older estimate of about 1440 for that manuscript is retained at witness level in the lineage record.

date_statements.display: Earliest witness verified in this research: MS. Rawl. B. 490, currently dated 1400–1410

assertion.notes: This is a witness date and terminus ante quem for the work, not a composition date. The full census of eight surviving English texts was not independently redated here.

date_statements.display: Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland, 1166–1185

places.label: English colonial community in late medieval Ireland
notes: A social and cultural audience region, not an exact modern administrative boundary or proven scribal locality.

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship places the vernacular production in Ireland; no town, institution, or patronal household has been securely identified.
The work was adapted for a late-medieval English colonial readership in Ireland; audience and place of copying remain distinct questions.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The work's Middle English is more specifically described in scholarship as Hiberno-Middle English. Latin and Greek in Furnivall's notes, quotations, and comparison material belong to editorial or source layers and are deliberately excluded from this work-language facet.

form_statements.description: Continuous vernacular historical prose divided into editorially regularized chapter units in the encoded edition.

genre_statements.label: Translation and adaptation
notes: The relation is both translational and adaptive: omission, reordering, condensation, and expansion are integral to the vernacular work.

subject_statements.label: Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland, 1166–1185
English colonial community and identity in medieval Ireland

summaries.value: The English Conquest of Ireland is an anonymous Hiberno-Middle English prose retelling of Gerald of Wales's Expugnatio Hibernica. It narrates the Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest from 1166 to 1185 for a late-medieval English readership in Ireland. The translator worked from an unidentified intermediate recension rather than a securely recovered Latin exemplar and reshaped the source through condensation, omission, reordering, and expansion while preserving its broad historical sequence and colonial perspective.

EngConIre encodes two complete manuscript versions of the same work: TCD MS 592 and MS. Rawl. B. 490. Furnivall printed them on facing pages, but the Michigan XML serializes the TCD version first and the Rawlinson version second. Its 124 chapter DIV2 elements therefore represent 62 editorial chapter positions repeated across two versions, not 124 independent works or necessarily 124 authorial divisions. A short TCD MS 593 specimen in the foreword and Furnivall's terminal note remain editorial paratext. The 1969 reprint's glossary and indexes were expressly excluded from the Michigan encoding.

The two printed witness streams are substantially parallel but are not interchangeable. Furnivall explains that each supplies omissions in the other and chose the Rawlinson text as closer to the standard written language. The XML preserves both streams, their page sequence, and most witness folio milestones, but some chapter headings, supplied passages, punctuation, and folio-marker placements are editorial judgments.

extent.description: One medieval prose work, represented in two complete encoded witness versions. Each version has 62 XML DIV2 chapter units, for 124 version-level divisions in the representation; the complete XML has 5 DIV1, 127 DIV2, 130 HEAD, 112 MILESTONE, 171 PB, 185 P, 196 NOTE1, 43 CORR, and 146 SUPPLIED elements. These are encoding and edition metrics, not a claim that the abstract work intrinsically has 124 chapters. The front and back editorial parts are retained with zero item counts, and the glossary and indexes are absent from the XML.

content_parts.label: English Conquest of Ireland
content_parts.summary: The single anonymous Hiberno-Middle English prose work, counted once although represented in two complete witness versions.
notes: The two child parts are versions of this one work and therefore have zero item counts.

content_parts.label: TCD MS 592 version
content_parts.summary: The complete version copied in the manuscript formerly cited as TCD E.2.31 and now cataloged as IE TCD MS 592, printed on the even pages of Furnivall's parallel-text edition.
notes: MS 592's adjacent f. 28r fragment of James Yonge's Secreta Secretorum dedication and a prayer are outside this work boundary.
The XML contains malformed late folio milestone values '42b' and '7b'; these are preserved as encoding defects, not accepted as manuscript foliation.

content_parts.label: MS. Rawl. B. 490 version
content_parts.summary: The complete version in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490, copied for Furnivall by Mrs Parker and printed on the odd pages of the parallel-text edition.

locators.value: ff. 1r–28r, inferred from Furnivall's edition and XML folio milestones

notes: The current Bodleian catalog identifies the English Conquest as the manuscript's first work but does not state its precise locus; the folio range here is an edition-to-encoding inference and remains qualified.
The manuscript also contains James Yonge's Secreta Secretorum translation, which is a separate adjacent work.

content_parts.label: Furnivall's editorial paratext
content_parts.summary: The retained title matter, dedication, forewords, third-manuscript specimen, and terminal note surrounding the two medieval witness streams.

locators.value: preliminary matter through p. xvi and terminal editorial note

notes: These modern editorial materials are represented because the XML retains them, but their language and prose are not facets or counted items of the medieval work.

content_parts.label: Title matter, dedication, and forewords
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's title matter, dedication, account of the manuscripts and collaborators, source discussion, historical framing, and editorial acknowledgments.

locators.value: title and dedication; foreword pp. vii–xiii

content_parts.label: TCD MS 593 comparison specimen
content_parts.summary: A short comparison specimen from the middle of TCD MS 593 p. 7, corresponding to chapter XIII in the two main edition witnesses; it is not a third complete encoded version.

locators.value: pp. xiv–xvi; compared with chapter XIII on pp. 30–35

notes: Furnivall described MS 593 (then F.4.4) as a later transcript of MS 592; the modern TCD record describes the textual relation more cautiously.

content_parts.label: The unenglisht last Chapters
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's terminal editorial note discussing material from the Latin source not rendered in the English versions.

related_works.title: Expugnatio Hibernica
notes: Gerald of Wales authored the Latin source work. The English Conquest appears to depend on an unidentified intermediate recension; no exact surviving Latin codex is asserted as the direct exemplar.
The English work is also an adaptation, but one translation_of relation is used here with the adaptive relationship stated in its genre and notes.

evidence.citation: General Corpus EngConIre source XML and linked source-lineage evidence.
evidence.locator: IDG, FILEDESC, ENCODINGDESC, REVDESCR, FRONT, BODY, BACK, DIV1, DIV2, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, NOTE1, P, CORR, and SUPPLIED
evidence.summary: Confirms the XML identity and source hash, the 1969 and 1896 bibliographic statements, two main witness streams, Furnivall's paratext, language and prose form, editorial caveats, and the encoded exclusions.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of EngConIre.xml recorded in the source-lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Verifies exact element counts; FRONT/BODY/BACK boundaries; two sequential 62-chapter witness versions; page and folio milestone ranges; the MS 593 specimen; terminal note; and malformed XML milestone values retained as encoding defects.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, English conquest of Ireland, item EngConIre.
evidence.summary: Confirms the stable item identifier, current title, Furnivall credit, collection placement, and present U.S. public-domain rights statement.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166–1185, EETS Original Series 107 (London, 1896).
evidence.locator: title page; foreword pp. vii–xvi; parallel text pp. 2–151; terminal note; glossary and indexes pp. 153–172
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for edition title and date, the two-witness layout, collaborator responsibilities, the Parker transcript and proof collation, witness relationships, the MS 593 specimen, Dimock consultation, editorial interventions, and the encoded work and paratext boundaries.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Dublin, IE TCD MS 592, Conquest of Ireland, DOI 10.48495/rn301607q.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current MS 592 shelfmark, the English Conquest locus ff. 1r–27v, adjacent f. 28r content, manuscript scope, catalog attribution wording, and current DOI identity. The DOI payload is metadata, not a downloaded facsimile.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Dublin, IE TCD MS 593, Conquest of Ireland, DOI 10.48495/th83m792c.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current MS 593 shelfmark and manuscript scope, the English Conquest on pp. 1–55, its stated relationship to MS 592, and Furnivall's pp. xiv–xvi specimen. The DOI payload is metadata, not a downloaded facsimile.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Western Medieval Manuscripts, MS. Rawl. B. 490, current TEI catalog record.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark and identity, 1400–1410 date, parchment support, English Conquest as the first work and abridged translation of Expugnatio Hibernica, separate James Yonge content, and Rawlinson provenance.

evidence.citation: Caoimhe Whelan, 'Translating the Expugnatio Hibernica: A Vernacular English History in Late Medieval Ireland,' in Text, Transmission, and Transformation in the European Middle Ages, 1000–1500 (Brepols, 2018), 165–192.
evidence.summary: Supports the anonymous vernacular translation and adaptation, Irish and colonial audience context, eight surviving English texts, lost original, intermediate-recension source model, exact-exemplar uncertainty, and adaptive textual strategies.

evidence.citation: Caoimhe Whelan, 'New Readers, Old History: Gerald of Wales and the Anglo-Norman Invasion of Ireland,' in Writing History in the Anglo-Norman World (York Medieval Press, 2018), 239–255.
evidence.summary: Supports the work's readership, colonial-historiographical function, reshaping of Gerald's conquest narrative, cultural setting, and the distinction between Gerald's Latin authorship and anonymous English vernacular production.

Provides the entity and relation graph, exact artifact fixity, edition and encoding chain, collaborator-mediated Rawlinson path, witness catalog caches, facsimile and access audit, rights layers, editorial practices, and unresolved questions used by this descriptive record.

open_questions.question: When and where in Ireland was the anonymous vernacular translation and adaptation made?
next_steps: Review the complete witness census and current dialectological and textual scholarship rather than assigning a witness's scribal date or language directly to the lost translation.
Test whether documentary, patronage, or network evidence narrows the broad late-medieval English colonial context.
Retain 1410 only as a terminus ante quem from the current Rawlinson manuscript date until stronger evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Which intermediate recension or lost Latin exemplar underlies the English Conquest?
next_steps: Compare all surviving English versions with the recensio and manuscript evidence for Expugnatio Hibernica.
Keep Dimock's printed Latin edition as Furnivall's editorial comparison source, not as the medieval translator's physical exemplar.
Do not promote Furnivall's resemblance to a particular Latin version into direct descent without a modern stemmatic argument.

open_questions.question: What is the documentary basis, if any, for attributing the English Conquest to Thomas Bray or associating it with a translation reportedly made for Primate Dowdall in 1551?
next_steps: Ask TCD catalogers for the sources behind the MS 592 title and description.
Trace older TCD catalogs and scholarship for Thomas Bray and Dowdall attribution history.
Continue to separate James Yonge's adjacent Secreta Secretorum translation from authorship of the English Conquest.

open_questions.question: How do all eight reported surviving English texts relate to one another, and are the two complete EngConIre witnesses representative of the full vernacular tradition?
next_steps: Build a current witness census with exact shelfmarks, loci, dates, completeness, and stable catalog identifiers.
Distinguish full copies, excerpts, later transcripts, and related notes before extending the part model.
Compare chapter boundaries and omissions without treating Furnivall's supplied chapter heads as authorial structure.

open_questions.question: What is the exact current cataloged folio locus of the English Conquest in MS. Rawl. B. 490?
next_steps: Request a current foliation and contents statement from the Bodleian or inspect the codex directly.
Check any supplied images against Furnivall's and the XML's folio milestones.
Keep ff. 1r–28r labeled as an edition-and-encoding inference until confirmed from the manuscript or a current catalog.

notes: The abstract work is counted once. TCD MS 592 and MS. Rawl. B. 490 are parallel witness-version parts with zero item counts, not two independent works.

Furnivall's title, chapter heads, supplied passages, punctuation, notes, and source comparisons belong to the edition layer. The XML further serializes a facing-page edition and includes explicit normalization and milestone-placement judgments.
The TCD MS 593 passage is only a foreword specimen. It is not modeled as a third complete encoded version and TCD MS 593 is not placed in the main source path.

Gerald of Wales is the author of Expugnatio Hibernica, not the English Conquest. James Yonge is associated with separate adjacent manuscript content, and the current Thomas Bray wording is retained only as disputed attribution history.
Latin and Greek editorial quotations and apparatus are excluded from the work language facets. Encoding element counts are representation metrics, not intrinsic intellectual structure.

The immediate 1969 Greenwood source was not physically inspected. The locally cached complete 1896 scan contains an unnumbered Henry Bradley correction leaf absent from the XML, leaving its presence in the 1969 reprint unresolved at the edition layer.
